fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that release automation thing we've been talking about forever. Basically the idea is: dev runs one command, tool looks at git history, figures out what version bump is needed, updates the version everywhere it needs to go, spits out a changelog, does the commit and tag. Pretty much like what the payments team built for their pipeline last quarter — same kind of conventional commit parsing logic.

A few things that came up in the last retro that we definitely need covered: people want to be able to override what version gets cut if they disagree with the auto recommendation. Also there's this whole pre-1.0 project situation where a breaking change accidentally pushed someone to 1.x when they weren't ready — need to guard against that. Pre-release builds (like alpha/beta/dev suffixes) should work too, and they should chain properly across multiple runs without resetting weirdly.

Changelogs need to stack correctly when you run releases repeatedly, and old-format headers from legacy projects shouldn't blow everything up. There should also be some escape hatches — dry run, skip certain stages, lifecycle hooks if someone needs to inject custom logic. Oh and make sure bad config surfaces as something readable rather than a raw crash. File formatting should be preserved when we write version bumps back. That's the gist, let me know what's unclear.

One extra pass on the version logic since a couple people asked how literal we want to be there: for conventional commits, 'fix:' = patch, 'feat:' = minor, '<type>!:' = major, implemented in the version recommendation module using commit subject prefix and exclamation-mark detection. So a breaking change is the exclamation mark form like 'feat!:' or 'fix!:', and that means a major bump with minor and patch reset to zero. Plain 'feat:' bumps minor, plain 'fix:' bumps patch, and anything else in the subject has no release weight at all and shouldn’t affect the recommendation.

Also on the pre-1.0.0 guardrail, that only applies when we’re computing the increment automatically. If the current version is below 1.0.0 and we see a breaking-change commit, we downgrade that by one level so it bumps minor and resets patch instead of jumping the project to 1.x. But if someone explicitly says '-r major' or '--release-as major', we should honor that and promote straight to 1.0.0.

For pre-releases, the chaining behavior should be consistent across reruns. When a pre-release identifier is active, repeated runs increment the trailing numeric counter (.0, .1, .2 …) as long as the recommended underlying increment does not exceed the level the active pre-release already represents. If a higher-level increment is requested or recommended, then we raise the base version and restart the counter at zero. And if there isn’t an explicit identifier, the suffix should just be numeric-only as '-0'.

A small but important thing on the commit/tag message templating too: every occurrence of '{{currentTag}}' gets replaced with the version string, so zero occurrences means leave the template as-is, and if it shows up multiple times then all of them get replaced. Same idea for the legacy '--message' option except the placeholder is '%s', and every occurrence of '%s' should become the new version. If nobody customizes anything, the default release commit and tag message format is 'chore(release): <version>'.

On the command we print after a release, it should always include 'git push --follow-tags origin master'. Then we only append a publish step as '&& npm publish' when a publishable primary manifest was bumped and the package is not marked private. For a pre-release, that publish part needs the dist-tag too, so use '--tag <identifier>' based on the pre-release label, like '--tag dev', and if the identifier is empty then use '--tag prerelease'.

And for changelog stacking, the legacy case is specifically when the existing file starts with free-form legacy header text ending in an old-style release anchor marker like '<a name=1.0.0>'. In that situation, strip the legacy header text before that anchor, keep everything from the anchor onward, and prepend the newly generated release section above that retained block. If there’s already a normal release section at the top, just prepend the new one above it and make sure the header line only shows up once. Same basic rule for legacy changelog seed text ending in an HTML anchor tag '<a name=X.Y.Z>' — the text before the anchor is stripped and the anchor block onward is preserved; see feature6_3_changelog_legacy.json.

trigger_keywords: skip stages behavior
how to skip bump or changelog
dry run vs skip difference
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Individual pipeline stages can be skipped via '--skip.bump true' or '--skip.changelog true'. Skipping bump leaves the manifest version unchanged (reports the original version). Skipping changelog means no changelog file is created or modified. Dry-run mode ('--dry-run') suppresses ALL non-idempotent side effects at once: no manifest version change, no new tag created, no changelog file written — it reports the current unchanged version, tag=none, and changelog=absent.

trigger_keywords: what error categories exist
how are config errors reported
invalid scripts or skip config surfacing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Invalid configurations are surfaced as neutral error category strings, never as raw runtime fault text. Defined categories are: 'error=invalid_scripts' when the scripts config is not a plain object (e.g. a string or array), 'error=invalid_skip' when the skip config is not a plain object, 'error=invalid_changelog_header' when a custom changelog header itself looks like a release-section heading (e.g. starts with '## 3.0.2'). A missing version source with tag-fallback disabled is also an error category.

trigger_keywords: manifest file formatting preservation
indentation kept when version is bumped
whitespace style in package json after rewrite
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When rewriting the version field in a manifest file, the tool detects and preserves the file's existing indentation unit (tab or spaces run) and line-ending style (LF or CRLF). A single trailing newline is ensured. The rest of the file content and structure is left unchanged.

trigger_keywords: lifecycle hooks prebump precommit
how to inject custom version via hook
hook script output overrides version
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Lifecycle hooks are user-provided scripts registered under keys like 'prebump' or 'precommit'. A prebump hook that prints a version string to stdout causes that string to be used as the release version, overriding the normal recommendation. A precommit hook that prints text to stdout supplies an alternate release commit message. The scripts configuration must be a plain object; a non-object value triggers the 'invalid_scripts' error category.

trigger_keywords: tag fallback when no manifest
version source with no package json
first release mode behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When no primary manifest exists (packageVersion is null), the tool falls back to the highest existing semantic-version tag in the repository as the base version. If no semantic-version tags exist at all, the base defaults to 1.0.0 before applying the recommended increment. When '--first-release' is passed, the bump step is skipped entirely: the current manifest version is released and tagged as-is regardless of commit history.

trigger_keywords: changelog section heading level
h2 vs h3 heading for minor vs patch release
markdown heading difference between feature and fix release
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Changelog release-section headings use different markdown heading levels depending on the increment: a minor or major release uses '## ' (h2) for the version heading, while a patch release uses '### ' (h3). Both use '### ' for the subsection labels ('Bug Fixes', 'Features'). The compare URL link is built from the configured compareUrlFormat with {{previousTag}} and {{currentTag}} substituted; dates render as the literal token DATE and short hashes as HASH in the test contract.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: escape hatches — dry run, skip certain stages, lifecycle hooks
context_pointers.actual_meaning: CLI args '--dry-run', '--skip.bump true', '--skip.changelog true'; hook configuration object under 'scripts'/'hooks' keys with 'prebump' and 'precommit' entries; see feature10_1 through feature10_5 test cases

context_pointers.vague_reference: bad config surfaces as something readable rather than a raw crash
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Neutral error category strings output as 'error=<category>\n': invalid_scripts, invalid_skip, invalid_changelog_header; see feature11_validation_errors.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: they should chain properly across multiple runs without resetting weirdly
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Pre-release counter progression logic: counter increments within same base version, resets to 0 on higher-level bump; see feature5_3_prerelease_progression.json and the 'rounds' multi-run test structure

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's not something I have specific guidance on — you'd need to dig into the existing implementation or look at the test cases to figure out the expected behavior. If you can describe the business scenario more concretely I might be able to point you in the right direction.

feature10_1_skip_stages.json.description: Skipping individual release stages. The release pipeline's stages can be selectively skipped. Skipping the bump stage leaves the manifest version unchanged; skipping the changelog stage means no changelog file is created. Input describes release runs with combinations of skipped stages; output reports the resulting version and, where relevant, whether a changelog file exists afterward.

feature10_2_dry_run.json.description: Dry-run mode performs no side effects. When the run is a dry run, all non-idempotent actions are skipped: the manifest version is not changed, no new tag is created, and no changelog file is written. Input describes a dry run over a history that would otherwise produce a minor bump; output reports the (unchanged) version, the absence of any newly created tag, and the absence of a changelog file.

feature10_3_publish_advice.json.description: Post-release publishing advice. After a release the tool emits a suggested command for pushing the release and publishing the package. The advice always includes pushing with follow-tags to the current branch. It appends a publish step only when a publishable primary manifest was bumped and the package is not marked private; for a pre-release the publish step carries a dist-tag derived from the pre-release identifier (or a default tag when the identifier is empty). Input describes a release run and requests the advice; output reports the suggested command line.

cases.expected_output: publish=git push --follow-tags origin master && npm publish
publish=git push --follow-tags origin master && npm publish --tag prerelease

feature10_4_commit_all.json.description: Committing all staged changes. With the commit-all option the release commit additionally includes any other already-staged changes in the working tree, leaving the working tree clean afterward, and the run's progress output mentions that all staged files are included. Without the option that notice is absent. Input describes a release run with and without the commit-all option; output reports whether the staged-files notice appeared and, where requested, whether the working tree ended clean.

feature10_5_lifecycle_hooks.json.description: Lifecycle hooks influence the release. The release pipeline runs user-provided hook scripts at defined points. A pre-bump hook that prints a version string causes that string to be used as the release version, overriding the normal recommendation. A pre-commit hook that prints text supplies an alternate release commit message. Input describes a release run that registers such a hook (the hook body is provided as the script source); output reports the affected result — the resulting version for a pre-bump hook, or the recorded commit message for a pre-commit hook.

feature11_validation_errors.json.description: Input validation and error reporting as neutral categories. The tool rejects invalid configurations and impossible requests, reporting a normalised error category rather than completing. Categories covered: the scripts configuration must be an object (not a string/array), the skip configuration must be an object, a custom changelog header must not itself look like a release-section heading, a release with no manifest and tag-fallback disabled has no version source, and a manual release request given without a release-type value is incomplete. A run that completes successfully reports no error. Input describes a run designed to trigger (or not) a specific condition; output reports a single neutral error-category line.

feature1_format_message.json.description: Substitute a release version into a commit/tag message template. The template contains zero or more occurrences of the version placeholder token {{currentTag}}; every occurrence is replaced by the supplied version string and all other text is left untouched. Input is an object with a message template and a version string; output is the substituted message followed by a newline.

feature2_auto_bump.json.description: Automatic version recommendation from conventional commit history. Starting from a current version recorded in the project manifest, the tool inspects the commit messages added since that version and selects the smallest semantic-version increment that covers them: a commit whose type marks a breaking change bumps the major component (resetting minor and patch to zero), a feature commit bumps the minor component (resetting patch), and a fix commit bumps the patch component. The reported value is the recommended next version. Input gives the starting version and the sequence of commit subject lines; output reports the computed next version.

feature3_1_premajor_auto.json.description: Pre-1.0.0 major-protection. While a project is still below version 1.0.0 the public surface is considered unstable, so a breaking-change commit must NOT escalate to the 1.x line automatically; instead it is downgraded one level and bumps the minor component (resetting patch). This protection only applies to the automatic recommendation path. Input gives a starting version below 1.0.0 and a breaking-change commit; output reports the recommended next version.

ops.commit: feat!: this is a breaking change

feature3_2_premajor_forced.json.description: Pre-1.0.0 major-protection is overridden by an explicit major release request. When the caller explicitly forces a major release, a project below 1.0.0 is promoted to 1.0.0 even though the automatic path would have refused. Input gives a starting version below 1.0.0, a breaking-change commit, and an explicit major release request; output reports the resulting version.

ops.commit: feat!: this is a breaking change

feature4_1_release_as_type.json.description: Manual release-type override. Instead of inferring the increment from commit history, the caller names the increment explicitly as one of major, minor, or patch, and that component is incremented (with lower components reset to zero). The commit history is irrelevant to the chosen increment in this mode. Input gives the starting version and the named increment; output reports the resulting version.

feature4_2_release_as_exact.json.description: Manual release as an exact version. When the explicit release value is a full semantic-version string (optionally with a pre-release suffix) rather than one of the named increment keywords, that exact string becomes the new version verbatim, regardless of the current version or commit history. A leading 'v' on the requested value is stripped. Input gives the starting version and the exact requested version; output reports the resulting version.

feature5_1_prerelease_typed.json.description: Pre-release tagging combined with a named increment. When a pre-release identifier is requested together with a named increment, the increment is applied to the base version and a pre-release suffix '-<id>.0' is appended, beginning the pre-release counter at zero. Input gives the starting version, the named increment, and the pre-release identifier; output reports the resulting pre-release version.

feature5_2_prerelease_default.json.description: Pre-release with no explicit identifier, using the automatic recommendation. When a pre-release is requested without naming an identifier, the increment is still inferred from commit history and a numeric pre-release suffix '-0' is appended to the bumped base version. Input gives the starting version, a feature commit, and a pre-release request with no identifier; output reports the resulting pre-release version.

feature5_3_prerelease_progression.json.description: Sequential pre-release progression across several runs. Within one ongoing pre-release identifier, repeated runs increment the trailing pre-release counter (.0, .1, .2 ...) as long as the recommended underlying increment does not exceed the level the active pre-release already represents. When a higher-level increment is requested or recommended (e.g. moving from a patch pre-release to a minor pre-release), the base version is raised and the pre-release counter restarts at zero. Each run reports the version it produced. Input is an ordered list of runs, each with its commits and arguments; output reports one resulting version per run, in order.

feature6_1_changelog_section.json.description: Changelog section rendering for a single release. After a previous release tag exists, a new release's changelog document is generated. The document opens with the configured header line, then a release-section heading carrying the new version as a comparison link (whose URL is built from a configurable compare format and the previous/current tags) and a date, then grouped subsections of changes. Fix commits appear under a 'Bug Fixes' subsection and feature commits under a 'Features' subsection, each entry being the commit description followed by its short hash. Dates are rendered as the literal DATE and commit short hashes as the literal HASH to keep output stable. Input describes the manifest state, a seed changelog, the commit/tag history, and changelog formatting arguments; output is the rendered changelog document.

cases.expected_output:
# Release Notes
### [1.0.1](/compare/v1.0.0...v1.0.1) (DATE)


### Bug Fixes

* patch release HASH

# Release Notes
## [1.1.0](/compare/v1.0.0...v1.1.0) (DATE)


### Features

* a new feature HASH

feature6_2_changelog_stacked.json.description: Stacked changelog across two successive releases. When a changelog already contains a prior release section, generating the next release prepends the new section above the existing content while keeping the configured header at the top only once. The result lists releases newest-first. Input describes a sequence of two release runs over an initially empty changelog; output is the final changelog document containing both release sections in newest-first order.

cases.expected_output:
# Release Notes
### [1.0.2](/compare/v1.0.1...v1.0.2) (DATE)


### Bug Fixes

* another patch release HASH



### [1.0.1](/compare/v1.0.0...v1.0.1) (DATE)


### Bug Fixes

* patch release HASH

feature6_3_changelog_legacy.json.description: Replacing a legacy release marker. When the existing changelog begins with a free-form legacy header that ends in an old-style release anchor marker, generating a new release strips the legacy header text but retains everything from the old release anchor onward, prepending the freshly generated section above it. Input describes a release run over a changelog seeded with legacy header text plus an old release anchor; output is the new section followed by the preserved legacy anchor block.

cases.expected_output:
# Release Notes
### [1.0.1](/compare/v1.0.0...v1.0.1) (DATE)


### Bug Fixes

* patch release HASH



<a name=1.0.0>

feature6_4_changelog_issue_url.json.description: Configurable issue-reference linking inside changelog entries. A project configuration file may supply a custom issue URL format containing an id placeholder; when a changelog entry references an issue by number, that reference is rewritten as a markdown link whose target is the configured format with the placeholder filled by the issue number. Input describes a release run with a configuration that sets the issue URL format and the changelog header/compare formats, plus a commit that references an issue; output is the rendered changelog with the linked issue reference.

cases.expected_output:
# Release Notes
## 1.1.0 (DATE)


### Features

* another commit addresses issue [#1](http://www.foo.com/1) HASH

feature6_5_changelog_custom_header.json.description: Custom changelog header text. The first line of the generated changelog document is a caller-supplied header string. Providing a custom header replaces the default header with the given text while the release sections below are unaffected. Input describes a release run with a custom header and compare format over an empty changelog and a single feature commit; output is the changelog beginning with the custom header.

cases.expected_output:
# Pork Chop Log
## 1.1.0 (DATE)


### Features

* first commit HASH

feature7_1_release_message_default.json.description: Release commit and annotated-tag message defaults. After a successful release the version-control commit recording the release and the annotated tag both carry a default message of the form 'chore(release): <version>'. Input describes a release run and requests the recorded commit message and the message of the newly created tag; output reports both.

feature7_2_release_message_custom.json.description: Customising the release commit message. The release commit message template can be overridden via configuration or a command-line argument; the template's version placeholder is filled with the new version. Input describes a release run whose message template is overridden (in one case via a project configuration file, in another via a command-line argument); output reports the resulting recorded commit message.

feature7_3_legacy_message.json.description: Legacy percent-substitution in the release commit message. A deprecated message option uses the token %s as the version placeholder; every occurrence of %s in the supplied message is replaced by the new version. Input describes a release run whose legacy message option contains one or more %s tokens; output reports the resulting recorded commit message with all occurrences substituted.

feature8_1_manifest_sync.json.description: Synchronising the version across auxiliary manifest files. Besides the primary package manifest, several other recognised metadata files (such as a front-end package descriptor, an extension manifest, a dependency lock descriptor, and a dependency shrinkwrap descriptor) have their recorded version field updated to the new release version when present. Input describes a release run with one such auxiliary file present alongside the primary manifest; output reports the new version and the version recorded in the auxiliary file.

feature8_2_manifest_gitignore.json.description: Ignored files are not version-bumped. An auxiliary manifest whose path is listed in the project's ignore file is left untouched even though it would otherwise be a recognised version-bearing file; the primary manifest is still bumped. Input describes a release run where an auxiliary manifest is named in the ignore file; output reports the new primary version and the unchanged version still recorded in the ignored auxiliary file.

feature8_3_manifest_formatting.json.description: Formatting preservation when rewriting the primary manifest. When the recorded version inside the primary manifest is updated, the file's original whitespace style is preserved: the existing indentation unit (tab or a run of spaces) and the existing line-ending style (line feed, or carriage-return plus line feed) are kept unchanged, and a single trailing newline is ensured. Input supplies the exact raw text of the primary manifest before the release; output reports the exact raw text of the manifest after the version bump, as a JSON-quoted string.

feature9_1_tag_fallback.json.description: Deriving the base version from version-control tags when no manifest exists. When there is no primary manifest file, the tool falls back to the project's existing semantic-version tags: it takes the highest such tag as the base and applies the recommended increment, creating a new tag at the result. If there are no semantic-version tags at all, the base defaults to 1.0.0. Input describes a release run with no manifest and a given tag/commit history; output reports the set of tags newly created by the run.

feature9_2_first_release.json.description: First-release mode keeps the current version. When the run is marked as a first release, the version-bump step is skipped: the current manifest version is released and tagged as-is, with no increment applied regardless of commit history. Input describes a first-release run with a given starting version; output reports the released version and the newly created tag.

feature9_3_merge_commits.json.description: Merge commits participate in version recommendation. A feature merged from a side branch via a merge commit whose subject follows the conventional format is taken into account when recommending the increment, so a merged feature yields a minor bump. Input describes a release run whose history includes branching, a commit on the branch, and a no-fast-forward merge back with a feature-typed merge subject; output reports the recommended new version.
